title: Measure doubling for small sets in SO(3,R).
speaker: Yifan Jing
abstract: Let SO(3,R) be the 3D-rotation group equipped with the real-manifold topology and the normalized Haar measure \mu. Confirming a conjecture by Breuillard and Green, we show that if A is an open subset of SO(3,R) with sufficiently small measure, then \mu(A^2) > 3.99 \mu(A). This is joint work with Chieu-Minh Tran (NUS) and Ruixiang Zhang (Berkeley).

proof_stages.label: Product-set framework and SO(3) small-doubling theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes product-set notation and Haar measure on SO(3), states the Breuillard-Green small-set conjecture, and states the proved result that sufficiently small open A satisfy mu(A^2) >= (4-epsilon)mu(A), with an asymmetric version.

proof_stages.label: Sharpness mechanism from one-dimensional stabilizers
proof_stages.summary: Neighborhoods of one-dimensional subgroups, made explicit via cap-preimages under SO(3) acting on S^2, show that the constant 4 can only be approached from below and lead to a stronger conjectural exact lower bound.

proof_stages.label: Broader compact/noncompact conjectural landscape
proof_stages.summary: The SO(3) result is placed among general compact Lie-group conjectures, known noncompact Brunn-Minkowski inequalities without an epsilon loss, curvature heuristics, SL(2,R) analogues, and links to convolution inequalities.

proof_stages.label: Existing ingredients and failure of direct noncompact methods
proof_stages.summary: The talk explains why the KAN-decomposition methods used in the noncompact case do not solve SO(3), and records a previous compact semisimple gap theorem giving a uniform small-set gain above doubling 2.

proof_stages.label: Contradiction sequence and normalized ultraproduct limit
proof_stages.summary: Assuming the theorem fails, one takes small counterexamples A_n in SO(3), rescales Haar measure so mu_n(A_n)=1, and forms an ultraproduct limit with finite A_infty, infinite ambient measure, and preserved small doubling.

proof_stages.label: Lie-model descent and noncompact gap forcing an R-quotient
proof_stages.summary: A Lie-model theorem is used to quotient the ultraproduct to a connected unimodular noncompact Lie group while retaining the projected 3.99-doubling bound, so the noncompact Brunn-Minkowski gap forces d-m=1 and hence a homomorphism to R with compact kernel.

proof_stages.label: Pullback of an interval and contradiction with the compact gap
proof_stages.summary: An interval in the R-quotient is pulled back to the ultraproduct, giving a set of doubling 2, and definability transfers it back to finite SO(3) sets of doubling 2+epsilon, contradicting the earlier uniform compact gap theorem.

proof_stages.label: Control of quantitative losses in the model-theoretic descent
proof_stages.summary: The hidden difficulties are that small doubling first produces only approximate groups and quotient/open-subgroup passages can destroy the sharp constant; random left-right translations and fiber-density/differentiation arguments are introduced to preserve the 3.99 bound through the descent.

examples.name: Spherical-cap preimages in SO(3)/SO(2)
examples.note: The sets A_theta={g: angle(z,gz)<theta} satisfy A_theta^2=A_{2theta} and have doubling tending to but staying below 4, showing why a 4-epsilon theorem is the sharp small-set form and suggesting the stronger conjectural profile.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker defines the cap-preimage A_theta by an angle condition, checks A_theta^2=A_{2theta}, uses the cap-volume formula, and computes the doubling ratio as 4 cos^2(theta/2).

passes.reason: the K-bi-invariant spherical-cap preimages are sharp model sets and are used to show an exact factor 4 cannot hold
The SO(2)-invariant spherical-cap preimages are highly regular tight examples with ratio 4cos^2(theta/2)<4, obstructing any exact 4 lower bound.
The K-bi-invariant spherical-cap preimages form the sharp small-doubling obstruction to an exact factor-4 bound.
The SO(3) preimages of spherical caps are rotationally symmetric tight examples with doubling 4cos^2(theta/2)<4, showing an exact 4 bound cannot hold.
The SO(2)-bi-invariant spherical-cap preimages are highly symmetric candidate extremizers and give ratio 4 cos^2(theta/2)<4, obstructing an exact 4 bound.
The K-bi-invariant spherical-cap preimages are highly symmetric and give the sharp 4cos²(θ/2)<4 obstruction to a uniform 4 bound.
The SO(2)-invariant spherical-cap preimages give the sharp curved family with ratio below 4, showing an exact 4 bound cannot hold.
The SO(2)-bi-invariant spherical-cap preimages give a tight curved model with doubling ratio 4cos^2(theta/2)<4, showing an exact 4 bound would fail.
The SO(2)-fibered spherical caps are rotationally symmetric sharp cap models with μ(Aθ²)/μ(Aθ)<4, so they obstruct an exact 4 bound.
The round SO(2)-invariant cap preimages satisfy A_theta^2=A_{2theta} and give the sharp curved obstruction to an exact factor-4 bound.
The SO(3) preimages of spherical caps are rotationally symmetric near-sharp minimal-doubling sets and are used to show a constant 4 bound cannot hold exactly.
the K-bi-invariant spherical-cap preimages satisfy A_theta^2=A_{2theta} and give the sharp curved obstruction to a universal factor 4

examples.name: Euclidean Brunn-Minkowski scaling
examples.note: The Euclidean Brunn-Minkowski inequality, especially the case A=B giving the factor 2^d, is recalled to make the expected factor 4 in an effective two-dimensional local model concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker recalls the Euclidean Brunn--Minkowski inequality and explicitly derives the A=B scaling factor 2^d, using d=2 to motivate the constant four.

passes.reason: the Euclidean A=B Brunn–Minkowski factor 2^d is the sharp scaling benchmark, not a refutation
The Euclidean Brunn-Minkowski case gives the sharp equality scaling factor 2^d used as a benchmark, not to refute a claim.
Euclidean Brunn-Minkowski gives the sharp 2^d scaling benchmark rather than refuting a claim.
Brunn-Minkowski with A=B gives the sharp Euclidean scaling factor 2^d used as the tight model for the constant.
Euclidean Brunn--Minkowski gives the sharp flat-space scaling factor 2^d used as a model, not as a failure case.
Flat Euclidean Brunn-Minkowski is the maximally regular model and the 2^d A=B scaling is sharp.
The flat Euclidean model is maximally regular and gives the sharp 2^d Brunn–Minkowski scaling motivating the factor 4, not a refutation.
The Euclidean Brunn–Minkowski case is used as the sharp 2^d scaling benchmark motivating the factor 4, not as a failure case.

examples.name: SL(2,R) hyperbolic-disk analogue
examples.note: For SL(2,R) acting on the hyperbolic plane, preimages of hyperbolic disks give a noncompact analogue with a 4-term plus curvature-dependent correction, motivating the broader noncompact conjectural formula.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker runs the analogous disk construction in the hyperbolic plane for SL(2,R), relates the terms to dimensions, and works through the normalization/parameter calculation in response to a question.

passes.reason: hyperbolic-disk preimages in SL(2,R)/SO(2) are rotationally symmetric sharp-model candidates for the noncompact conjecture
Hyperbolic disks in SL(2,R)/SO(2) are symmetric model sets proposed to realize the sharp noncompact formula, not deployed as a failure case.
The hyperbolic-disk preimages are radial symmetric balls presented as the sharp model for the noncompact curvature-corrected bound.

examples.name: Kunze-Stein indicator-function comparison
examples.note: The Kunze-Stein convolution estimate is specialized by taking f=g=1_A to compare set-doubling bounds with L^p convolution phenomena in nonabelian harmonic analysis.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker plugs indicator functions into the Kunze--Stein convolution inequality and derives a resulting lower bound for mu(A^2) via Hölder and p approaching 2.

passes.reason: indicator functions in the Kunze-Stein comparison are ordinary test functions used only to relate analytic and set estimates

examples.name: KAN decomposition for SL(2,R) and its cover
examples.note: The KAN decomposition makes the previous noncompact proof look like an induction over T x R x R, while the universal cover and the compact SO(3) case illustrate why this method does not transfer directly.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker writes out the KAN factors for SL(2,R), identifies them as torus/real-line pieces, compares with the universal cover, and reasons through why this decomposition helps in the noncompact case but not SO(3).

passes.reason: the SL(2,R) KAN decomposition and especially its universal cover are used to obstruct the naive induction-style proof idea
The KAN example is standard structure, while the universal cover’s infinite center is used as an obstruction to the naive induction/finite-center argument.
The universal cover in the KAN discussion obstructs the naive induction or finite-center extension despite looking like R×R×R.
The KAN decomposition is standard, while the universal cover’s infinite center is invoked as an obstruction to a naive induction/finite-center argument.
The standard KAN decomposition and the universal-cover case are used to show the naive induction/compact-transfer method fails because the cover has infinite center.
The SL(2,R) KAN case is the rank-one minimal semisimple base case, and its infinite-center universal cover obstructs the naive KAN-induction.
The universal-cover KAN case is used as an obstruction to the naive KAN-induction or compact-transfer argument, while the decomposition itself is standard.
